speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five. January 28, 1996 with Mrs. Sonia Hochman. And I'm Maryann [INAUDIBLE]. So you originally decided to go to-- to Israel. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened was the head of the Betar was my school teacher's husband, Mrs. [? Rookstein. ?] His name was Herr [? Rookstein. ?] And he came home one day, and he told her, you know, Sarah Dodek is going to be on our next transport to go to Israel. She got panicky.

speaker: interviewee
text: She called my mother. And she said to her, Frau Dodek-- that means-- and she spoke to her in like a German, Jewish. She said, Frau Dodek, do you know where your Sarah is going? Because my Hebrew name is Sarah. She says she's going to Israel. She'll be in a [YIDDISH]. She'll be in a orphanage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she'll wait till I'll come there, and she'll get an education. She says, I don't know if she's going to be able to get an education. I don't know what the situation is going to be. She lost a child that I reminded her of in Auschwitz. And when-- in class, she always used to stand and stare at me. And she loved me so dearly. She'd hug me and kiss me, thinking that she was hugging and kissing her own child. I remind her very much of her child. She says, if I would be you, I would hold on to this child. I wouldn't let her go by herself to Israel. You don't know what's going to happen to her. Because she knew already there was going to be fighting. Her husband was the head of the Betar in our camp. And it was-- so my mother stopped me from going to Israel. A month later, we saw children were registering to go to America from the orphanage. So I decided I'm going to go to America. I felt I'm 13 years old already. I need an education. If my father would be alive, I would get a proper education. I would go to gymnasium, you know, to high school. I would go to university, I would go to college. Here, I'll come with my mother's quota. We knew it was going to be like in three years or in four years. I'll be already 16 years old, 17 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are going to be my chances? I'll go work in a factory. My father, I felt never-- he would've wanted me to have an education. Did not envision for me to be working in a factory. What will become of my sister and of my mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided I'm going to America. So my mother agreed with me. My grandma did it too. Because we felt I'll be in an orphanage in the America, and I'll-- I'll get an education. By the time they will come, I'll know the language. I'll know how to write. I'll know how to read. And I'll have a chance to make some kind of a life for myself. I registered to go to America. A few weeks later, they call me. About a week or two later, they call me and to interview me. I came in. A man started to ask me questions. He spoke to me in Jewish. But there was an American man also there. And they were like-- it was like an interpreter. And then he said to me, just a minute. And they bring in two women. And they sit me down near a table. And they take out an attache case. And in this attache case, they had white paper with a lot of black marks on them. It looked like a different type of pictures. And they started to ask me what I think these pictures are. What it looks like to me. I should interpret whatever is on this black-- the design on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ink blots.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ink blots. OK. I started to interpret, and they asked me what I learned in school. And I started to sing for them and recite poetry for them. And I had a very good memory, so my poetry-- my poems-- I was reciting long poems for them. They couldn't believe themselves that I-- the memory that I had all that. And I started to sing for them. And I told them I want an education. They asked me why. I said because I want to live again. I've lost all my childhood. I want to be a child again. These two women take me, and they said come. I'm going! What am I-- you know, I'm in their hands. They tell me come. They take me straight to the consulate. And they tell the consulate-- go in and they speak to a man. And they said this child need priority. In two weeks, I was in the Funkkaserne in Munich. This was in the barracks in [INAUDIBLE] in Munich, where the people, they added me with a big transport of people. And from there we went to Bremen Hafen. I remember my mother came with my grandmother and my sister, and my aunts came to say goodbye to me because I was already remained in the Funkkaserne. And in fact, the head from the Funkkserne promised these ladies-- they must have been American social workers-- that they would-- he would take care of me. Because I was like the youngest, because there were other groups of orphans. But I was like the youngest. And they didn't want me to be with a grown. Because the other were already hugging and kissing. They had already boyfriends. And they didn't want me to-- they felt I was still too young. So he used to take me home at night. Because we-- that week or two I was in-- in Munich, I went to see the doctors. They had to take x-rays of me, and all kind of tests. They took blood tests and all kind of things. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the first time you would have been separated actually from your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself. But-- but I knew that they will come to America one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew that your father would have liked you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Get an education.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So when did you come to America? What day?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 22, 1947. I arrived on It was a army ship to New York with a group-- with another group of children. We were a big transport. We came to Caldwell Avenue in the Bronx.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a receiving place. And they start to ask you questions. Where you would like to be sent. Where you would like to go? And I want to stay in New York. I'm afraid if I leave New York, my mother won't find me. Anyway, one day my grandmother's sister comes in, and-- and she-- and she says-- she asked the home, when is the mother and the sister going to come? They said they didn't know I had a mother and sister. They were not really aware of anything here on this side of the channel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- and I started to cry. I don't want to leave New York. So first some relatives took me. Things that didn't work out. And then I belonged to the foster home. I was in the foster home until my mother came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what school did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Public School 202, Miss [? Boyer. ?] I have a picture of her, of the graduation from the eighth grade. She was my mentor. She was a-- a Christian lady here in the United States. At that time when I came here, I only thought the Jews were helping Jews. But she was a Christian lady, a school teacher. An Irish lady I think, [? Boyer. ?] And she became my mentor. She used to keep me after school and help me read and help me write. And I used to sing very well. And she had me in the glee club. She wanted to-- to integrate me among the children, that I should feel the same like the rest of the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she know of your history?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. There was a Jewish schoolteacher, a woman. She was like the assistant principal. And I explained to her, and she explained to her in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they believe you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. They were shocked, but they believed me. They were shocked. Anyway, I graduate public school. And I went in-- in-- in 1949, in January, I graduated public school, and I went in to high school. I went in to Thomas Jefferson High School. And I was living in a foster home. And every two or three months, I used to go on Tremont Avenue. They had the Jewish foster home there. And I would have to report to Miss [? Kagan. ?] She was my social worker. And I remember one time, it was Halloween, and all the children were running around with apples, and with, you know, with the uniforms, with-- you know, and I was going to report to my social worker. And I was crying because I missed my mother, and I missed my sister and my grandmother. And I was lonely. And the foster home wasn't that easy, but I was happy that I could get an education. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1949, in November, my grandmother came. My cousin Claire tells me that we went to see my grandmother when she was getting off the boat, you know, before the people could see, I jumped across the gate. I don't even remember it. And I grabbed my grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandma took a look at me. She says to me, what happened to you? I'm so depressed. [CRYING] Because I used to write to my mother I'm taking piano lessons. I'm taking ballet lessons. It wasn't the truth, but I wanted her to feel good. She says to me, wherever I'll be? I'll sleep in the street. You'll sleep in the street. But the foster home wouldn't give me to my grandmother because she didn't have a home. And in order to give it to me, my grandmother had to have a room for me. So I remained in the foster home, but I could go to visit my grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: On December 2, 1949, my mother arrived with my sister to Boston. And she writes me to meet her at Grand Central Station. I go to Grand Central Station to meet my mother. I take a look, my mother was walking, dressed beautifully, nice. My sister already has ponytails. And I see a man with my mother. My mother had remarried almost two-- more than two years, but I was not aware of it. And she says to me in Polish, this is your [POLISH], this is your uncle. So I realized my mother was married. She married [? Isaac ?] [? Baron ?] from Vilna. My mother took me back from the home, from the foster home. We started to live normal life. I went to high school, my sister started school. My mother and stepfather struggled an awful lot, but they worked hard. They worked very hard. And they gave us-- My grandmother came to live with us, my Uncle Sam came to live with us. And they worked hard. They did not believe in any charity. They didn't take anything from anyone. They were very proud. And somehow they work themselves up. In 1949, there was a wedding. And my grandmother make my Uncle Sam take me to a wedding. That's when I met my husband, Harold Hochman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was also a survivor. He lived through an awful lot. He lived, I would say, 20, 30 miles away from my town. Not too far away, maybe an hour with a car. When the Germans came in-- he was also in the Tarnopol District.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they did the same thing in our town what they did in his town. They made a ghetto. Then in the ghetto, they came in, they pulled out 20 people. And one of these 20 people was his mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they counted one, two, one, two. Every other person they shot. His mother was shot. Then he also lived through the big action that they took people to Belzec. His-- most of his family was taken away. He and his father-- his father's name was [INAUDIBLE] Hochman, his mother's name was [INAUDIBLE] Hochman, his sister's name was [INAUDIBLE] Hochman, [INAUDIBLE] Hochman.

speaker: interviewer
text: All these people died.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Hochman, and Esther Hochman, a little sister, and my husband name is [INAUDIBLE] Hochman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's the only one alive in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he thought he was the only one. I'll tell you. He ran away, and they were hiding. And he was caught with his father and brother. And he ran, and the police ran after the father and brother. They shot the father and brother, and he ran away. Then he found his sister. She was hiding with the little sister, Esther. And they were in hiding together a few months by a peasant. One day the peasant came and said to them get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they get out. And they asked the little sister, Esther-- to try-- they taught her how to say the Ukrainian prayers-- to try to go and tell the peasants that she's an orphan from a different village. And maybe somebody will have mercy and take her in. And he and his sister went to another peasant, and she allowed him to stay. This is just before liberation, '44. And this is Esther, is the one who somehow stayed alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but for 16 years my husband didn't known that she was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll get to it. Let me just finish this off. My husband is caught with his sister. He's caught with his sister, the Germans, a little boy says, Jude on them. And he's caught. And they're in a barn. They keep him in a barn. And his sister says to him try to run away. Maybe you run very fast. Maybe-- she was older than him. She was the oldest of the children. Maybe you can run away. Because his brother [INAUDIBLE], and the father was shot already. And she knows that she's going to be killed. She doesn't know if Esther is alive or not. She thinks maybe-- maybe this brother [? Hersh, ?] maybe Harold can survive. And he runs away. He tells the-- the Germans that he has to go to the bathroom to make under the barn, and he runs away. After liberation-- I'm condensing it, because I know-- after liberation, he found his father's brother, Simon Hochman, with three sons, Eli Hochman, [? Isaac ?] Hochman, and Harold Hochman. They took him in and they raised him like a son. Now I'm getting back to 1952. In 1952, I'm still met this boy in 1949, but I have to finish high school. My mother says to me-- I want to get married. My mother says to me, I sent you to America to have an education. Well, I graduated high school in June of 1952, and I got married in 1952. In 1953, the entire Hochman family bought homes in Bayside. We were like the first Holocaust survivors to move out on the islands. We all build homes. My husband was already in business. In 1954, I gave birth to my first child, Florence. In '55, I gave birth to Linda. And in '58, I gave birth to Mark. Today, Florence is a fine educated lady, young lady. My daughter Linda is married now to Dr. Richard Smook and they have two children, Liza Joy and Andrew. My son Mark is Dr. Mark Hochman, and he's married to Dr. Claudia Hochman. And they have a little girl, Emily Hochman. And I'm very, very proud. In between my sister got married in 1968. She got-- 1958, my sister got married in Montreal. Her name is Sima Blitzer now. She is married to David Blitzer. And she has two daughters. The daughter name is Eta. Her name is now Eta Shaul, and she has three children, Daniel, Henia Eliana, and Jonathan. Her younger daughter is Bonnie Blitzer Stone. She married Elihu Stone from Brookline, Massachusetts, and they have two children, Juliana and Yehuda. And we're very, very grateful to God that we survived. But the rest of my family, my aunts and uncles, the Stermer family, settled in Montreal. They became developers. They built complete-- they built communities. They are very, very comfortable people. Our children, we gave the best of educations.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the matriarch in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother was here till I got married, until my sister got married. And then she moved to Montreal. My mother died in 19-- my grandmother died in January-- December. Sorry. Sorry, I'm mixing up dates. December 19, 1983. She was 96 years old. My grandmother lived a long life. Until about 95, she was full-- she used to sit in the wheel chair in a home, an extended home, with her book in her hands, and all kind of different newspapers. She would read German, Jewish, English. Brilliant lady. My mother died of cancer on April 12, 1984.

speaker: interviewer
text: She lived in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: New York, yes. Yes. I lived in Bayside till 1976. In 1976, I moved to Great Neck, Long Island. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is how life went down. I'm very grateful to United States for embracing my family, for allowing myself and my family to get an education. As a child, I suffered an awful lot. But as a grown up, I had all the freedoms that everyone else had.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you maintain your ability to enjoy life? That it isn't affected by the Holocaust completely?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was affected. My husband had terrible nightmares. Now I'll get to a part of my husband's nightmares. I was married, we lived in Bayside, and life was wonderful. Except reoccurring nightmares to my husband. His mother came to him in dreams that he should go and search for his sister. He was tormented by it. One day in 1958, a distant relative of his came from Toronto. And she told us that she's corresponding with a Ukrainian man. And he helped save them. That she's corresponding with them, and she sent some packages. I says to her-- [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] her name is-- [INAUDIBLE] do me a favor. Write to him. Let him go into villages and look. Maybe Esther is alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Harold, he-- he-- he changes pajamas twice a night. He-- he has nightmares. He's suffering. She says to me, [NON-ENGLISH]. Child, dear child, if she would be alive, wouldn't she have come out? I says please, do me a favor. Write to him. Four weeks later, we get a letter. And he says if I find her, what will I have? Of course we write him. I didn't know how to write Ukrainian. My mother started to write letters for me. He writes back, we'll make you a millionaire. Then we get a-- a few weeks later, we get a picture. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went in forest, and he found Esther. She looked just like his mother. And he told her that she has a brother, she has an uncle, and cousins. And they're in America, and they're millionaires. You know, in-- because we wrote him. We'll do anything for him. And she's going on a Christian papers. And they tell her-- they tell her to go to church Sunday, and he will meet her on the road going to church. And not to say nothing to the woman, because he was afraid the woman will kill him. Because they were still killing people. This was 1958. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: '58, '59, something like that. '58. Yes, '58. And she went to church. He took her away to Tarnopol. And now you have to make for [INAUDIBLE] Esther Hochman. How do you do that? And it's Russian. My husband's uncle, Simon Hochman, went to Poland, and he found Polish people that went to court and swore that they knew there was a [INAUDIBLE] Hochman, and had a child, Esther Hochman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took two years to get her to Poland. We get her to Poland, and the Berlin blockade comes on 1960. The Iron Curtain comes down. Here she was safe. In Ukraine here, we made her into a Jew. And who knows what will happen to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to Congressman Seymour Halpern. And we told him our story. And he introduced a bill of hardship in the United States Congress. And it took another two years till we got her here to the United States. And where is she now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther lives in Brooklyn. She got married. She has two children. Her son is a lawyer. Her daughter married [? Eric ?] [? Stuckley. ?] Her husband, unfortunately, passed away. And she's a wonderful-- she was in business. She made a wonderful life for herself. And my husband has a sister. And we were always grateful to Congressman Seymour Halpern for helping us bring her here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful story. Let me just ask you one question. You grew up very fast. And as a child you were taught that you'll always have to look for another exit to escape. Are you looking for exits?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You know. I became such a stationary person, that I live in my house in Bayside almost 24 years. And I'm living here almost 20 years. We're going to be married June 21, it's going to be 44 years that my husband and I are married. We got married in June, 21, 1952. And in June '96, the 21st is going to be 44 years that we're married. I go to the same butcher. I go to the same grocery. I'm so glad not to make any changes. I'm so glad to be an American, and to raise my children as Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything I didn't ask which you would like to add and finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would tell-- like to tell you also my-- we-- in 1952, after I was married, another miracle happened. My father had a sister that lived in Czernowitz in Romania. I don't know, she ran from Czernowitz to Russia. From Russia with her husband, from Russia they ran to Asia. They went up in Iran. From Iran to Pakistan. They wound up in Bombay, India. In the summer of 1952-- and her husband got killed on the way of running-- my grandmother was in Montreal. She went for the summer to see her other children in Montreal. She calls me. She says to me, you won't believe it. A miracle happened. Your father says that Chanzel is alive. And she has a child of six or seven years old. Her name is Paulene. And she says, and they're not letting her in. She came from Bombay, India. They might send her back to India. And we don't know if you'll ever see her again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the following day, I went to Canada. I took a sleeper at night, and I went to Montreal. My father had first cousins there, [INAUDIBLE] and the there [? Rosenblatts. ?] And we went to Quebec City.

speaker: interviewee
text: They made all the connections. And my Aunt Chanzel Mates and her daughter Paulene remained in Canada. Then I found that my grandmother [INAUDIBLE] had two brothers in Los Angeles. The [? Rosenblatt ?] brothers. A year later, they brought her to Los Angeles. Paulene got married to Izy Shanfeld, and they have two children, Linda and Michael. Linda got married. In fact, my sister and I, with our husbands, went to the wedding, to Los Angeles. To [? Ari ?] [? Lichtenberg ?] from Mexico City. And they were living in Los Angeles, in Beverly Hills. Unfortunately, my cousin got cancer, and she died in 1989, December the 7th. And my aunt got a stroke and died December 21, the same year. And they're buried next to each other in Los Angeles in the cemetery. But-- but-- but their grandchildren, they're going to be great grandchildren, from my grandparents, from the Dodek family. So in a way, there was a miracle that she survived. It was a tragedy that Paulene died. She was 45 years old. Was beautiful young lady. And this is my life. This is the life of Sonia Dodek Hochman. And I hope to God that my grandchildren will be able to tell this to their children, their grandchildren. And they'll tell it of their grandparents' life. And I pray that they will be courageous, and that these things will never happen again. That no children should have to go through what I and my sister went through. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taking in Korolowka when my Uncle Nisel, if you noticed, he's on the left side, the first one. He was an uhlan. That's in the Polish cavalry. When he came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's an officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an officer in the Polish army, yes. No, he was in the cavalry. And next to it is my aunt Hannah. Then there's my grandmother, Ester. The front is my grandmother, Ester. My grandfather, [INAUDIBLE], my mother Henia Stermer Dodek and I'm the little girl, Sonia Dodek. On top there, you'll see boy with a white collar is Sam Stermer. Then [? Shulker ?] Stermer, then my father, Fischel Dodek. And then it's my Aunt [? Yetta. ?] This picture was taken in 1937, and I was about three years old. This picture is of my mother and father just before their wedding. This is Fischel and Henia Stermer Dodek. It was taken in 1932. This picture was taken in 1945 in Bytom in Poland, when we arrived to Bytom. To the right is my little sister, [? Simala. ?] I am Sonia. Then there is a boy, [INAUDIBLE], his name was [INAUDIBLE]. Then is my cousin, [INAUDIBLE]. And then there is a cousin of mine, [INAUDIBLE] Dodek who lives in Israel. And these were to orphan girls from our town. Their name was [? Chekta-- ?] I don't-- the surname, I remember. [? Chekta. ?] They were two orphans. They also are in Israel. This was the first picture that my sister and I took after the war. Before we go to the DP camp. This is a picture at the end of 1946, the beginning of '47. This was the orphanage, the kinderheim in Foehrenwald. And I am sitting to the left. My sister is the first little girl there. And then I am there. And these are children that survived the Holocaust by themselves. People brought them. They were in the cloisters, they were hiding by peasants. If you'll notice, most of these little girls have very Slavic faces. And beautiful girls, but with very sad eyes. These are the children that survived. And after the war, people brought them out from Christian homes to bring in to try to make Jews out of them so somebody would remain from that family. This picture was taken in the end of March in Foehrenwald in the DP camp, when I leave to go to America. If you notice, there is snow on-- on the the background. But Mama wanted to have a picture of me before I leave. So it's myself, to the right, my mother Henia, and my sister [? Simala. ?] This is how I looked when I came to the United States.
